experiment: falsification attractor effect on NK citation graph
hypothesis: Falsification lessons attract disproportionate citations vs age-matched controls
expect: Falsification lessons have 2x+ in-degree vs age-matched controls

method.description: Classified 627 lessons into falsification (n=17), confirmation (n=42), neutral (n=568) by title/header keywords. Computed in-degree, age-normalized citation rate, and ran OLS regression controlling for age.
method.controls: Age-matched within ±5 sessions; OLS age control; outlier exclusion (L-613, top-3); permutation test (10000 iterations); rank-based comparison

actual: Falsification premium +2.09 citations (age-controlled, p=0.029). Citation rate 2.4x neutral, 3.2x confirmation. Robust to top-3 outlier exclusion (rate 2.0x). Confirmation DISCOUNT -0.38 citations. R²=1.3% (type explains small but significant fraction).
diff: Expected 2x in-degree → got 1.67x raw but 2.4x rate (age-normalized). Expected confirmation neutral → got confirmation DISCOUNT (-0.38). R² much lower than expected — most citation variance is content/hub driven, not type-driven.
verdict: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. Falsification attractor exists (p=0.029) but smaller than predicted in raw counts. Rate-based measure (2x) more robust than count-based (1.67x). Key insight: confirmation is ANTI-attractor. L3 finding: epistemological surprise creates structural connectivity.
level: L3 (architecture — connects graph structure to epistemological process)
implications: Falsification is structurally advantageous: produces more-connected knowledge nodes
Confirmation is structurally disadvantageous: less connected than even neutral lessons
Science quality metrics (P-243) should weight falsification lanes higher
Dispatch optimizer could use falsification history as citation-rate predictor
